findings.title: Admin routes have no authentication dependency
findings.description: The admin router is included in the FastAPI app without any authentication dependency (Depends()). All admin endpoints including user listing and deletion are publicly accessible.
findings.recommendation: Add a Depends() security dependency to the admin router or individual endpoints. Use OAuth2PasswordBearer or HTTPBearer for token validation.

findings.title: User endpoints lack ownership verification
findings.description: The GET and PUT handlers for /users/{user_id} accept any user ID without verifying that the requester owns or has permission to access that record. Any user can read or modify any other user's profile.
findings.recommendation: Add authentication dependency and verify that the authenticated user matches the requested user_id or has admin privileges.

findings.title: CORS allows all origins with credentials
findings.description: CORS middleware is configured with allow_origins=['*'] and allow_credentials=True. This combination allows any website to make authenticated cross-origin requests to the API, enabling CSRF-like attacks.
findings.recommendation: Restrict allow_origins to specific trusted domains. Do not use '*' with allow_credentials=True.

findings.title: Debug mode enabled in production-style app
findings.description: The FastAPI app is initialized with debug=True, which exposes detailed error information including stack traces to clients.
findings.recommendation: Set debug=False for production. Use environment variables to control debug mode per environment.

findings.title: SQL injection via f-string interpolation in user lookup
findings.description: The user lookup query uses f-string interpolation to embed the user_id directly into a SQL string. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via the user_id path parameter.
findings.recommendation: Use SQLAlchemy parameterized queries with bound parameters instead of f-string interpolation.

findings.title: SQL injection in user update
findings.description: The update handler builds a SQL UPDATE statement using f-string interpolation with user-supplied name, bio, and user_id values. All three parameters are injectable.
findings.recommendation: Use SQLAlchemy ORM operations or parameterized queries for all database writes.

findings.title: SQL injection in admin delete
findings.description: The admin delete endpoint builds a DELETE query using f-string interpolation with the user_id path parameter. Combined with the missing auth check, this allows unauthenticated arbitrary data deletion.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries and add authentication/authorization checks.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing
findings.description: Passwords are hashed using MD5 (hashlib.md5), which is cryptographically broken for password storage. MD5 hashes can be reversed via rainbow tables or brute force in seconds.
findings.recommendation: Use bcrypt, argon2, or scrypt via passlib. Replace hashlib.md5 with passlib.hash.bcrypt.hash().

findings.title: JWT with hardcoded weak secret and no expiration
findings.description: JWT tokens are signed with the hardcoded secret 'secret123' and have no expiration claim. Tokens are valid indefinitely and the secret is trivially guessable.
findings.recommendation: Use a strong random secret from environment variables. Add an exp claim to all tokens. Consider short-lived access tokens with refresh token rotation.

findings.title: Database error details leaked to client
findings.description: Exception handlers in the user routes return raw error messages including database error details to the client via str(e) in JSON responses. This leaks internal schema and query information.
findings.recommendation: Return generic error messages to clients. Log detailed errors server-side.

findings.title: Bare except blocks swallow errors silently
findings.description: Multiple bare except blocks catch and silently discard all exceptions, including the update handler and fetch-avatar endpoint. Errors are hidden, making debugging impossible and masking security issues.
findings.recommendation: Catch specific exception types. Log all exceptions. Return appropriate error responses.
